title: A Conditional Expanse
author: Ulysses
medium: Astro/SSG · client-side bigram (first-order Markov) engine + live measurement · substrate is eight verified sentences from Gertrude Stein's How to Write (1931), each cited, brought as local data.json
embodies: Runs Nathan Jones's reading of Stein's How to Write as a machine that 'uses the current word as the sole determining factor for what the next one should be' — a bigram process that 'anticipates the logic (and forms of error) of predictive text.' A transition table is built at load time from Stein's own sentences; it IS her 'conditional expanse' (the set of words she let follow each word). Two temperatures of the one rule: AUTOCOMPLETE (always the most-probable next word) collapses from any start into the loop 'a sentence a sentence …' — the stammer Jones hears, and the same low-diversity attractor as model collapse; STEIN (seeded sampling) stays locally grammatical yet falls apart semantically and dead-ends. Then it measures the real sentences: at 68 branch points Stein swerves from the most-probable word 39 times (57%), using the bigram form while defeating its predictability. The swerve is the construction (Tynianov). The engine, the collapse, and the count run in the browser; nothing is simulated. Deterministic: same seed, same walk. Limit disclosed: eight sentences is a demonstration corpus, not a population statistic.
